text: The surgeon begins by incising the posterior rectus sheath just lateral to the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal space and bringing down the preperitoneal fat. The incision progresses carefully to avoid bowel injury, revealing a prior suture near the hernia sac. The surgeon completes the division of the posterior rectus sheath down to the arcuate line, attempting to stay extraperitoneal.

The surgeon works on the hernia sac, attempting to bring down a thick sac, though it remains thin. The dissection progresses outside the hernia sac, aiming for a thicker layer. The omentum is visible herniating between the anterior rectus sheath and the rectus muscle, incarcerated in the anterior rectus space.

The surgeon places ports on the left side to access the abdominal wall anterior to the rectus muscle. The omentum is completely removed from the anterior rectus space. The surgeon proceeds to partially take down the large hernia sac, preserving material for posterior coverage. The sac is kept with the peritoneum or posterior sheath. The surgeon removes available peritoneum from the right rectus muscle and enters the right retrorectus space. The posterior rectus sheath is opened along the linea alba, starting at the arcuate line, using a grasper to incise about a centimeter away from the linea alba.

The surgeon dissects the areolar space within the posterior rectus space, ensuring the fibril areolar tissue remains anterior to preserve the neurovascular bundles. The dissection extends into the pelvis, developing the space of Retzius. The right round ligament is divided away from the internal ring to prevent injury to the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve.

The surgeon divides the left round ligament and performs a limited bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the right side. The posterior rectus sheath is divided approximately one centimeter medial to the linea semilunaris. The peritoneum is pushed off the posterior rectus sheath, and the entire hernia defect is visible. Image inversion is used to close the defect.

The surgeon uses image inversion to facilitate suturing of the hernia defect, ensuring the anti-erectus sheath and rectus muscle are included in the closure. The left side of the screen shows the patient's right rectus complex, with the anti-erectus sheath visibly separated from the rectus muscle. Multiple bites are taken from the large hernia sac, and sutures are laid out to evenly distribute tension along the incision, aiding in the re-approximation of the linea alba, including a small umbilical hernia.

The surgeon uses an OV lock suture to close the defect, then reverts the image to focus on the posterior closure. The posterior layer is closed using a V-Lock suture technique, with the surgeon managing excess tissue to ensure a proper closure. The closure of the posterior layer is nearly complete.

The surgeon measures the floor space for mesh placement, noting dimensions of approximately 27 centimeters in length and 19 centimeters in width. A medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh is unrolled and positioned to cover the entire floor. The mesh is secured to Cooper's ligament and the pubis using several interrupted vicryl sutures to prevent it from shifting. The mesh is visible covering the port sites.
